message:
**1 replication attempt of this paper has been recorded in the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA).**



> Efendić et al. (2021). Risky Therefore Not Beneficial: Replication and Extension of Finucane et al.’s (2000) Affect Heuristic Experiment. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 13(7), 1173–1184. https://doi.org/10.1177/19485506211056761
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** successful
> **Quote:** In two well-powered studies, using a more stringent analytic strategy, we replicated the original finding. We observed a strong negative relationship between judgments of risks and benefits across three technologies, although we do find that there was no change in risks when highlighting low benefits.
> **Quote source:** abstract


Manual inspection may help to assess what led to the described outcome. Future research can learn from replications and build on them. More information about why and how we submitted this automated comment can be found [here](https://github.com/forrtproject/flora-pubpeer/blob/main/README.md)

RepNote Team

**2 replication attempts of this paper have been recorded in the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA).**


**Replication 1**

> Klein et al. (2018). Many Labs 2: Investigating Variation in Replicability Across Samples and Settings. Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 1(4), 443–490. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245918810225
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** failed
> **Quote:** This result does not support the hypothesis that sales estimates are influenced by incidental anchors. The effect size was in the same direction as the original effect size, but much smaller (d = 0.04, 95% CI = [−0.01, 0.09], vs. original d = 0.30) and indistinguishable from zero.
> **Quote source:** pg. 16


**Replication 2**

> Shanks et al. (2020). Do Incidental Environmental Anchors Bias Consumers’ Price Estimations? Collabra: Psychology, 6(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.310
> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** failed
> **Quote:** No statistically significant evidence of incidental anchoring was obtained. In contrast, robust standard anchoring effects were found. The results suggest that anchoring is limited to situations which require explicit thinking about the anchor.
> **Quote source:** abstract


Manual inspection may help to assess what led to the described outcome. Future research can learn from replications and build on them. More information about why and how we submitted this automated comment can be found [here](https://github.com/forrtproject/flora-pubpeer/blob/main/README.md)

> 
> **The authors reported the replication as:** successful
> **Quote:** The coordinates of participants’ clicks on the fictional map were recorded (x, y) from the top left of the image and then recentered in the analysis such that clicks in the north half of the map were positive and clicks in the southern half of the map were negative. Across all samples (N = 6,591), participants in the high- SES condition (M = 11.70, SD = 84.31) selected a further north location than did participants in the low-SES con- dition (M = −22.70, SD = 88.78), t(6554.05) = 16.12, p = 2.15e−57, d = 0.40, 95% CI = [0.35, 0.45].
> **Quote source:** p. 12

> **The authors reported the replication as:** failed
> **Quote:** "The final (Stage 2) sample for the replication consisted of 265 total participants (136 in the powerful condition, 129 in the powerless condition, thus meeting our preregistered sample size of 126 per experimental group). Again, mean ECS score for the powerful group (M = 3.21, SD = 0.40) were descriptively larger compared to the powerless group (M = 3.17, SD = 0.36). To follow this up, I conducted a mini mixed-effects meta-analysis using the lme4 R package based on the data from Woltin et al.’s original study and our replication data, modelling study identity as a random factor. Again, there was no significant meta-analytic effect of condition on ECS scores, B = 0.01, 95% CI [-0.08, 0.11], p = .759."
